elements.sent-2411-001: Billions of individuals engage with Online Social Networks (OSN) daily.
elements.sent-2411-002: The owners of OSN try to meet the demands of their end-users while complying with business necessities.
elements.sent-2411-003: Such necessities may, however, lead to the adoption of restrictive data access policies that hinder research activities from ``external'' scientists---who may, in turn, resort to other means (e.g., rely on static datasets) for their studies.
elements.sent-2411-004: Given the abundance of literature on OSN, we---as academics---should take a step back and reflect on what we have done so far, after having written thousands of papers on OSN.
elements.sent-2411-005: This is the first paper that provides a holistic outlook to the entire body of research that focused on OSN---since the seminal work by~\citet{acquisti2006imagined .}
elements.sent-2411-006: First, we search through over 1 million peer-reviewed publications, and derive \revision{13,842 papers that focus on OSN: we organize the metadata of these works in the \dataset\ dataset, the first of its kind---which we publicly release.}

elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, as a constructive step to guide future research, we carry out an expert survey (n=50) with established scientists in this field, and coalesce suggestions to improve the status quo---such as an increased involvement of OSN owners.
elements.sent-2411-009: Doing so would improve the overall OSN ecosystem, benefiting both their owners and end-users---and, hence, our society.
elements.sent-2411-010: Online Social Networks (OSN) are platforms where end-users create virtual connections, share their experiences, communicate, and build social relations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Since the rollout of major OSN in the late 2000s -- such as Facebook and Twitter -- OSN have established a non-negligible space in the routines of billions of human beings~\citep{kim2018social .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, we now use OSN also for purposes revolving around aspects of our professional life.
elements.sent-2411-013: Examples include following and publishing news, finding work opportunities, purchasing or selling items, or even for full-time and OSN-specific jobs like ``social media manager'' and ``social influencer''~\citep{jin2019instafamous .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The growth of OSN was simply impossible to overlook, and the research community did not stand idly by.
elements.sent-2411-015: Starting from the seminal work by~\citet{acquisti2006imagined on Facebook, over the years thousands of scientific papers have been published, focusing on a plethora of themes revolving around various OSN and having plenty of societal implications.}
elements.sent-2411-016: For instance, ~\citet{chu2010tweeting started to design algorithms to detect ``bots'' in OSN, and years later,~\citet{bessi2016social found that such bots played a crucial role in the outcome of the US elections.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Since then, abundant papers have focused on detecting fake news~\citep{shu2017fake .}
elements.sent-2411-018: To sustain any OSN there is the effort of thousands of employees (at various levels), who work to provide a fulfilling experience to end-users while sustainably generating revenue for their own companies, and ensuring compliance with modern norms (e.g., regulations and moral customs).
elements.sent-2411-019: Although some OSN owners may appreciate collaborating with researchers, some decisions may also put ``barriers'' that are difficult to overcome by academics---such as allowing access to OSN data only through thousands-of-USD-worth of fees~\citep{verge2023twitter .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The interplay among these three stakeholders (OSN owners, researchers, and end-users) delineates a complex ecosystem.
elements.sent-2411-021: Ideally, the aggregated contributions of every player should improve the ecosystem as a whole---which would ultimately lead to an overall enhancement of our quality of life.
elements.sent-2411-022: As researchers, we want to facilitate such an improvement.
elements.sent-2411-023: To this purpose, we ask ourselves a broad question: ``what has happened since the first research paper on OSN?'' The OSN ecosystem has made enormous advances also thanks to researchers: in this context, are there some elements that have received a greater (potentially overshadowing others) degree of attention---and, if so, why?.

elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we perform a reflective exercise and seek to scrutinize the entire body of OSN research.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our goal is to examine whether prior literature, as a whole, conceals certain ``trends'' which, if not known, could be harmful to future research---and understand potential root causes.
elements.sent-2411-026: To this purpose, we first systematically collect the metadata of over 1 million publications, and identify \revision{13,842 works related to OSN.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We organize these papers in the \dataset\ dataset, the first of its kind for OSN-research, which we publicly release.
elements.sent-2411-028: Next, through a meta-analysis, we investigate our \dataset\ dataset across four dimensions: OSN prevalence, wherein we show that some OSN are significantly more studied than others \revision{(see Figure~\ref{fig:OSN-popularity-over-time ) ; topic popularity, wherein we reveal the topmost research topics considered in prior work; author analysis, wherein we elucidate some aspects of the authors of the papers in \dataset; regulation and policies, wherein we attempt to explain some of our findings in light of major changes, unrelated to research but which deeply affect OSN-related research.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Finally, to further validate some of the ``issues'' we uncovered, and to improve the status quo, we reach out to some of the authors of the papers in \dataset: through an (anonymous) expert survey, we collect the opinions of 50 renowned OSN researchers, highlighting the (often hidden) difficulties of carrying out impacting research in the OSN domain.

elements.sent-2411-049: Among our major contributions, we introduce the \dataset~dataset.

elements.sent-2411-052: We design \dataset\ with the intention of collecting ``all'' OSN-related and peer-reviewed papers (in computer science) that have ever been published.
elements.sent-2411-053: Hence, the first step entails identifying research-focused venues (i.e., journals and conference proceedings) that contain such publications.
elements.sent-2411-054: We begin our search with Google Scholar (among the most well-known repositories of scientific articles), and consider the subcategories within the ``Engineering and Science'' category~\ref{google-scholar-cats .}
elements.sent-2411-055: For each subcategory, Google Scholar lists the 20 most popular venues.
elements.sent-2411-056: We integrate these 20 top-tier venues with all those listed in the Springer's Lecture Notes in Computer Science and the International Conference Proceedings Series of the Association of Computing Machinery.
elements.sent-2411-057: We manually review all identified venues, remove duplicates and merge venues that changed publisher or name (e.g., WWW changed to TheWebConf).
elements.sent-2411-058: In total, we identify 135 relevant venues \revision{(reported in the Appendix) .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Then, for each venue, we query the Scopus database for all papers that appeared therein---starting from 2006 (the year in which Facebook was released to the general public)\revision{, as also done by~\citet{antonakaki2021survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: For every paper, we collect its metadata, i.e. title, authors (and affiliations), abstract, publication date, DOI.
elements.sent-2411-061: In total, we extract over 1 million ``candidate'' papers.
elements.sent-2411-063: There is no straightforward way to determine if any given paper is related to \revision{(any) OSN.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Hence, we design a sensible \revision{(and original) heuristic: for each paper (among our 1+ million), we automatically analyze its abstract, looking for mentions of the name of at least one OSN (intuitively, a paper on OSN ``should'' mention an OSN in its abstract).}

elements.sent-2411-080: Let us explain our procedure, which is systematic in nature.. Topic modeling.
elements.sent-2411-081: The main idea of topic modeling is to use a model to identify ``topics'' (i.e., thematic patterns) in a collection of documents---which, in our case, are the abstracts of the papers in \dataset{ .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Our model of choice is the well-known BERTopic~\citep{grootendorst2022bertopic , which we apply through the following steps: { (i) ~we create a sentence embedding for each abstract by using ``bge-small-en-v1.5'' (one of the best open-source models~\ref{link:Huggingface leaderboard ); { (ii) ~apply dimensionality reduction to such sentence embeddings via UMAP~\citep{mcinnes2018umap ; { (iii) ~cluster the reduced sentence embeddings via HDBSCAN~\citep{campello2013density ; { (iv) ~create topic representations based on the resulting clusters, i.e., we identify the most relevant words describing the topics clustered, which we do by using Count Vectorizer, removing English stopwords, and applying c-TF-IDF; { (v) ~finally, we assign a label on the resulting topics by using LLAMA-2~\citep{touvron2023llama .\footnote{\revision{Trial-and-Error and Alternatives.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The procedure described above was derived after extensive testing of various alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-084: e.g., for step { (i) ~we considered also ``all-mpnet-base-v2'' and ``bge-small-en''; for { (ii) ~we considered also PCA; for { (iii) ~we considered also K-Means; and for { (v) ~we considered KeyBert and MMR.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Specifically, we use LLM in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-086: First, as we mentioned, we use LLAMA-2 for topic labeling (i.e., ``given a set of keywords that denote a topic, what is a name that can denote them?''); we do this by issuing the prompt in Listing~2 (in the Appendix).
elements.sent-2411-087: Second, we use GPT-4 to describe the content of each topic identified by BERTopic (i.e., ``given the keywords and the label of a topic, what is a human-readable description of such a topic?''); we do this by issuing the prompt in Listing~1.

elements.sent-2411-265: To make our analysis feasible and meaningful, we first identify researchers that co-authored at least 5 papers in our \dataset: this criterion was met by 1,138 authors.
elements.sent-2411-266: We then analyze the most recent publications of these authors to pinpoint the most plausible contact information---which we find after manually reviewing 788 papers.
elements.sent-2411-267: Additionally, recognizing the value of diverse perspectives, we include emails of co-authors of these 788 papers, given that contact authors often differ from the primary author of interest.

elements.sent-2411-269: In total, we contacted 2,367 authors via an anonymous online questionnaire.
elements.sent-2411-270: Our questionnaire is anonymous, but we ask for some basic (and optional) information about each participant, such as age, country, and employment status; we also inquire about the participant's research background, such as which OSN they work on, years of experience in OSN research, and if they are still actively researching this field.
elements.sent-2411-271: We report a copy of the survey in our repository.
elements.sent-2411-272: The questionnaire has the following structure:.
elements.sent-2411-273: Data Gathering: Ideal vs Real World.
elements.sent-2411-274: We ask authors to rate (1=less relevant to 5=more relevant) what option they would prefer when gathering data for OSN research.
elements.sent-2411-275: We specifically mention using open-source datasets, collecting new datasets through official APIs, collecting datasets through methods not involving APIs, and entering contact with OSN organizations.
elements.sent-2411-276: Importantly, for this part, we first ask participants to provide their answer by assuming an ``ideal world'' (i.e., with no constraints or unlimited resources), and then ask the same question but by considering the implications of the real world.
elements.sent-2411-278: We ask closed questions about the authors' perceptions of the difficulty in collaborating with OSN providers and whether a more straightforward process to (i) partner up with OSN providers and (ii) obtain OSN data would facilitate future research.
elements.sent-2411-279: Reproducibility and Outlook We ask closed questions about the authors' perception of the degree of reproducibility of prior research on OSN.
elements.sent-2411-280: The questionnaire ends with an open question in which we ask for comments and feedback on the current state of OSN research.
elements.sent-2411-281: We sent emails to 2,367 authors in May 2024 (we only sent one email to avoid annoying fellow researchers) and collected responses for one week.
elements.sent-2411-282: In total, we received 50 answers (a participation rate of 2\%).
elements.sent-2411-283: We acknowledge our sample is small (we do not claim generality) but their opinion is still useful in driving future research.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our participants are mainly from Europe (64\%), North America (18\%), and Asia (12\%); such a distribution may create bias (e.g., Europeans are more affected by GDPR) in their responses.
elements.sent-2411-285: Most respondents are associate professors or above (74\%), stressing that the answers we received come from experts in the field.
elements.sent-2411-286: Indeed, 86\% of participants have been doing research for more than 5 years, while only 4\% is relatively new to the field, with less than 2 years of experience (but their opinion is still scientifically valid).

elements.sent-2411-302: Our investigation highlights that researchers encounter substantial challenges in studying OSN---a problem that is exacerbated by the numerous restrictions that OSN owners enacted over time.
elements.sent-2411-303: To fix these issues, our findings should become a subject of discussion not only among stakeholders within the OSN ecosystem (i.e., researchers and OSN owners\footnote{We argue that research on OSN should transcend simplistic (but fair and legitimate) visions such as ``career trajectories of researchers'' or ``a yet another source of profit for OSN owners.'' ), but also among policymakers.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Given the billion-sized userbase of OSN, and the potential of OSN to influence our society, a smooth development of such platforms should be within the interest of political institutions.
elements.sent-2411-305: According to our study, researchers have been advocating for such a change---but their ``voices'' have not been adequately heard, it appears.
elements.sent-2411-306: Throughout the course of our study, we identified numerous indicators suggesting that the priorities of researchers may not be fully aligned with the real world.
elements.sent-2411-307: The most evident sign of this phenomenon can be appreciated in Figure~\ref{fig:OSN-popularity-over-time , showing that Twitter has been the most represented OSN in research since 2011, despite being outside the top-3 OSN (popularity-wise) since 2015.}
elements.sent-2411-308: Such an asymmetry is particularly detrimental to younger demographics (who tend to use OSN that are less prevalent in research, such as Instagram and TikTok), as potential risks remain unexplored due to researchers’ propensity to focus on OSN that are ``easier to study'' -- despite the fact that such OSN may not fully capture the entire spectrum of our society.
elements.sent-2411-309: Let us elaborate on our previous claim.
elements.sent-2411-310: Consider the heatmap in Figure~\ref{fig:osn_topics : Twitter is a key resource in 10 out of the 17 most commonly studied research topics.}
elements.sent-2411-311: Such a finding suggests that Twitter is---or rather, was---a cornerstone for OSN research, owing to its open API and policies that permit straightforward access to its data, thereby fostering the collection and distribution of new datasets to the community.
elements.sent-2411-312: Such a characteristic, however, is not valid anymore: the easy accessibility to Twitter's data was curtailed in April 2023 with the introduction of (expensive!) paid tiers in its API.
elements.sent-2411-313: In contrast, other OSN, such as Instagram and TikTok, have always posed challenges to researchers interested in analyzing their data.
elements.sent-2411-314: We stress, however, that there is nothing wrong in carrying out research on an ``overly-studied'' OSN.
elements.sent-2411-315: However, it is factual that some OSN have a different user base, and lack of research on such platforms is detrimental to their communities (and, hence, to our whole society).
elements.sent-2411-316: Our findings have also highlighted a decreased ``interest'' from researchers towards OSN---despite the skyrocketing growth of these platforms' userbase.
elements.sent-2411-317: Indeed, starting from 2018, less researchers has been attracted in OSN research, and less peer-reviewed papers on OSN have appeared (see RQ1.a, RQ2.a, RQ4).

elements.sent-2411-388: Lastly, we perform a manual inspection: for each OSN (out of 296), we inspect up to 50 matched papers, discarding those that, despite meeting the above-mentioned criteria, do not focus on OSN.
